title: Vector Magnetometry with Broadband Microwave Fields in Nitrogen-Vacancy Centers in Diamond
authors: Tom R. Rieckmann, Arezoo Afshar, Aaron Z. Goldberg, Lilian Childress, Stefan Scheel, Khabat Heshami
abstract: We present a novel method for full vector magnetometry using nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers. In contrast to conventional optically detected magnetic resonance techniques, our method employs two distinct broadband microwave pulses and measures them after transmission through the NV sensor medium, thus capturing the line splitting of the ground state triplet due to the Zeeman effect. Two orthogonally polarized microwave pulses allow resolving all magnetic field components independently by reading out differently oriented NV centers. Simulated data is analyzed using deep neural networks, whose efficacy we expect to translate very well to experiments. Our method yields sensitivities between $5~\mathrm{pT}/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$ and $100~\mathrm{pT}/\sqrt{\mathrm{Hz}}$ across different magnetic field vector components, while achieving approximately $\mathrm{nT}$ accuracy at a signal-to-noise (SNR) ratio of $70~\mathrm{dB}$. By being capable of accurately measuring magnetic fields down to $25~\mathrm{μT}$, the need for a bias field beyond Earth's magnetic field is eliminated.
summary: This paper presents a breakthrough method for full vector magnetometry using Nitrogen-Vacancy centers in diamond. It overcomes the limitations of single-polarization measurements by employing two orthogonal microwave pulses and analyzing the resulting signals with deep neural networks. The technique achieves high sensitivity, enabling accurate magnetic field measurements down to the microtesla range without needing an external bias field.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of performing full vector magnetometry ($	ext{B}_x, 	ext{B}_y, 	ext{B}_z$) using Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) centers in diamond.
main_result: A novel method utilizing two orthogonally polarized microwave pulses and deep neural networks achieves high sensitivity ($\sim 5-100 	ext{ pT}/\sqrt{	ext{Hz}}$) and accuracy ($	ext{nT}$) for measuring the full magnetic field vector.
method: The technique involves measuring the transmission of broadband microwave pulses through the NV ensemble, which is then analyzed using deep neural networks trained on simulated data.
model_or_system: The physical system is the NV center in diamond, whose spin Hamiltonian governs the magnetic response. The measurement relies on analyzing the spectral line splitting induced by the Zeeman effect using two distinct, orthogonally polarized microwave fields.
key_observables: Measured microwave signal intensities ($I_{MW1}(t), I_{MW2}(t)$) and the estimated magnetic field components ($	ext{B}_x, 	ext{B}_y, 	ext{B}_z$).
important_parameters: Sensitivities ranging from $5 	ext{ pT}/\sqrt{	ext{Hz}}$ to $100 	ext{ pT}/\sqrt{	ext{Hz}}$; measurement down to $25 	ext{ }\mu	ext{T}$; $	ext{SNR}$ of $70 	ext{ dB}$.
main_assumptions: The method requires using two orthogonal microwave polarizations to resolve all three magnetic field components independently; the analysis relies on training deep neural networks on simulated data.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the frequency-domain response of NV ensembles, the dependence of estimation statistics ($\sigma_{i,est}$ and Bias) on the true field magnitude $|\mathbf{B}_{true}|$, and the improvement gained by using two microwave polarizations over one.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theory for vector magnetometry using two MW polarizations, details the inverse problem solving via deep neural networks, analyzes the performance metrics (sensitivity, bias) as a function of the true field strength, and discusses limitations and potential improvements.
why_interesting: This work bridges quantum sensing (NV centers) with advanced machine learning techniques (NNs) to solve a complex inverse problem in condensed matter physics, providing a robust, high-precision measurement tool.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Frequency-domain response of the NV ensembles at a magnetic field of |B| = 200 µT for two orthogonal mi- crowave polarization components. The first polarization vector, bMW1 (solid lines), is aligned along the +z-axis (θ1 = 0, ϕ1 = 0) with unit amplitude. The second polar- ization vector, bMW2 (dashed lines), lies in the transverse xy-plane with θ2 = π/2 and ϕ2 = 7π/12, also with unit amplitude. Each set of curves corresponds to a different field orientation (θ, ϕ), illustrating the distinct spectral re- sponses associated with the two linear MW polarization directions. The inset provides a zoomed-out view over the broader frequency range, illustrating the full resonance structure...

FIG. 2. Concatenated signals IMW1(t) and IMW2(t) on the vertical axis after preprocessing for different ϕ with |B| = 100µT and θ = 3π/8. The red dashed lines show symmetry axes for the local behavior around them.

FIG. 3. Depiction of NN-based Vector Magnetometry. Apart from a few simple preprocessing steps, the NN is tasked with directly giving Bx, By, Bz, |B| as output. The weights of the NN are updated during training using the mean square error between the true and estimated B.

FIG. 5. σi,est and Bias of the estimation for all Bi with an SNR of 70 dB in Method A. Each data point is extracted from a distribution of a series of estimations at the same Btrue, as depicted in Figure 4. One can see σi,est strongly depends on |Btrue|, whereas the Bias is mostly indepen- dent. Values for the minimum, maximum, and median of σi,est are given in Table I.
FIG. 4. Example histogram from the estimation of |B| using 500 different noisy samples at each SNR.
